content: Human brains compress long-term memory via activity-dependent pruning that is actively guided by curiosity-driven learning signals, a mechanism that can be adapted to build more efficient artificial agent memory systems that only retain high-value, context-relevant compressed memories. Artificial memory compression frameworks designed for long-term agent retention can in turn test how curiosity-gated compression explains human long-term memory forgetting patterns.
source_topics: [Memory compression and summarization for long-term retention]
memory as active learning human brain mechanisms
trigger_topic: [Memory compression and summarization for long-term retention]
